summary: Legacy validated record without machine-checkable validation proof. Controls the AutoShareServer and AutoShareWks values under LanmanServer. Disabling automatic administrative shares can reduce remote administration exposure, but it can also break workflows and tools that expect the built-in shares to exist.

setting.name: Automatic Administrative Shares
setting.casual_explanation: Windows automatically creates hidden administrative shares such as C$ and ADMIN$. This setting decides whether those built-in shares are created automatically.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable automatic administrative shares on hardened systems that do not want these built-in remote-management entry points. They keep them enabled when they rely on remote administration tools, scripts, backup jobs, or enterprise management workflows.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning automatic administrative shares off reduces one remote-management surface, but many management tasks and tools expect those shares to exist.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents AutoShareServer and AutoShareWks under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters. If the values do not exist, Windows uses the default behavior and automatically creates administrative shares. Setting the values to 0 stops automatic creation, but this does not remove IPC$ or manually created shares.

allowed_values.label: Automatic share creation enabled by default
allowed_values.meaning: Windows automatically creates the server administrative shares when no explicit override is present.

allowed_values.label: Do not create server administrative shares
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not automatically create server administrative shares.

allowed_values.label: Create server administrative shares
allowed_values.meaning: Windows creates server administrative shares automatically.

allowed_values.label: Automatic share creation enabled by default
allowed_values.meaning: Windows automatically creates workstation administrative shares when no explicit override is present.

allowed_values.label: Do not create workstation administrative shares
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not automatically create workstation administrative shares.

allowed_values.label: Create workstation administrative shares
allowed_values.meaning: Windows creates workstation administrative shares automatically.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes AutoShareServer = 0 and AutoShareWks = 0, which matches Microsoft's documented way to stop automatic administrative share creation.

windows_defaults.label: Windows default administrative-share behavior
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows client and server systems with LanmanServer enabled

states.rationale: When the value does not exist, Windows uses the default behavior and creates administrative shares automatically.
When the value does not exist, Windows uses the default behavior and creates administrative shares automatically.

windows_defaults.notes: This record documents the automatic shares only. IPC$ and manually created shares are outside this toggle.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Backup or management workflows that expect admin shares
avoid_for: Highly locked-down systems that intentionally forbid the built-in shares

states.rationale: Leave the explicit server override absent.
Leave the explicit workstation override absent.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You use normal Windows remote administration or do not want to break tools that depend on admin shares.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You specifically want to disable the built-in administrative shares for hardening.
tradeoffs: The built-in administrative shares remain available.

recommended_profiles.label: No default admin shares
intended_for: Machines that do not need remote admin-share access
avoid_for: Backup tools that expect ADMIN$ or drive shares

states.rationale: Disable automatic creation of server administrative shares.
Disable automatic creation of workstation administrative shares.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You understand the management tradeoffs and want to remove these automatic shares as part of a stricter baseline.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You still rely on built-in shares for administration, deployment, backup, or troubleshooting.
tradeoffs: Some tools and administrative workflows may stop working.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: remove administrative shares
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents AutoShareServer under LanmanServer\Parameters and states that 0 stops automatic creation of administrative shares.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: administrative shares are missing
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents both AutoShareServer and AutoShareWks under LanmanServer\Parameters and states that if the values do not exist, Windows automatically creates the administrative shares.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: ADMX_MSS-legacy / Pol_MSS_AutoShareWks
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the workstation administrative-share policy surface for the feature area and confirms AutoShareWks as the workstation control name.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes AutoShareServer = 0 and AutoShareWks = 0 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents AutoShareServer and AutoShareWks under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters and explains that setting them to 0 stops automatic creation of administrative shares while missing values keep the default automatic behavior. The app writes those exact documented values on the same documented path, with no unresolved control-surface mismatch.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters\AutoShareServer` ... `0`; `AutoShareWks`; `If the AutoShareServer (server) or AutoShareWks (workstation) registry entry is set to 0, Windows won't automatically create administrative shares.`
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft directly documents AutoShareServer and AutoShareWks under the LanmanServer Parameters path and explains that setting them to 0 prevents automatic administrative share creation. The app writes AutoShareServer = 0 and AutoShareWks = 0 on that exact documented path.
